era_context: The High Middle Ages in Europe; the Investiture Controversy between Pope Gregory VII and Emperor Henry IV at its peak. The Norman Conquest of England is 10 years past; the First Crusade is 20 years away. In the Islamic world, the Seljuk Empire dominates Anatolia and the Levant following their victory at Manzikert (1071). In East Asia, the Northern Song Dynasty rules China; the Liao Dynasty controls the north. Japan is in the Heian period under the Fujiwara regency.

events.title: Synod of Worms: Henry IV deposes Pope Gregory VII
events.description: On 24 January 1076, King Henry IV of the Salian dynasty, supported by German bishops, convened the Synod of Worms and declared Pope Gregory VII deposed, claiming the pope had overstepped his authority. This was the climactic moment of the Investiture Controversy—the struggle over whether secular rulers or the papacy controlled the appointment of bishops. Gregory VII had excommunicated Henry in 1075 for investing bishops without papal consent. The synod's declaration was a direct challenge to papal supremacy and marked the escalation of a conflict that would define European politics for decades.
key_figures: Henry IV (King of the Romans, later Holy Roman Emperor)
Anno II (Archbishop of Cologne, advisor to Henry)

sources.name: Bonizo of Sutri, Liber ad amicum (contemporary polemical account)
Historiographic consensus (modern scholarship on the Investiture Controversy)

events.certainty_note: The date and event are well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The synod's proceedings are documented in multiple chronicles.

events.title: Pope Gregory VII excommunicates Henry IV in response
events.description: In response to the Synod of Worms, Pope Gregory VII excommunicated Henry IV a second time (the first excommunication was in 1075). Gregory also released Henry's subjects from their oaths of allegiance, effectively delegitimizing his rule. This counter-excommunication escalated the conflict and forced Henry to seek reconciliation to restore his political authority. The mutual excommunications represent the peak of the Investiture Controversy and demonstrate the papacy's willingness to use its spiritual authority as a political weapon.

sources.name: Register of Pope Gregory VII (papal records)

events.certainty_note: The excommunication is documented in Gregory's own register and multiple contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Death of Sweyn II Estridsson of Denmark
events.description: Sweyn II Estridsson, King of Denmark, died in 1076 after a long reign marked by conflicts with Norway and the Holy Roman Empire. His death led to a succession dispute between his sons, particularly Harald III Hen and Canute IV, which would dominate Danish politics for the next decade. Sweyn had consolidated Danish power and expanded its influence in the Baltic, but his death initiated a period of internal instability.

sources.name: Adam of Bremen, Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum (near-contemporary chronicle)
Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (later compilation, 13th century)

events.certainty_note: The death date is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary Scandinavian sources, though some sources vary slightly on the exact year.

events.title: Seljuk Turks consolidate control of Anatolia after Manzikert
events.description: Five years after their decisive victory at Manzikert (1071), the Seljuk Turks under the Sultanate of Rum continued to consolidate their control over Anatolia, displacing Byzantine authority and establishing Turkish settlement patterns that would persist for centuries. By 1076, Seljuk control extended from the Aegean to the Euphrates. This transformation fundamentally altered the demographic and political character of Anatolia, shifting it from a Byzantine Greek-speaking region to a Turkish-speaking Islamic one. The process was gradual but accelerating, with Byzantine attempts at reconquest largely unsuccessful.
key_figures: Alp Arslan (Seljuk Sultan, died 1072, but his legacy dominated)

sources.name: Anna Komnena, Alexiad (Byzantine chronicle, early 12th century)
Archaeological evidence of settlement patterns and fortifications

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Seljuk consolidation is well-established, but specific events in 1076 are not individually documented. The date represents a point in a continuous process.

events.title: Northern Song Dynasty: Wang Anshi's New Policies face resistance
events.description: In 1076, Wang Anshi, the reformist chief minister of the Northern Song Dynasty under Emperor Shenzong, continued to face fierce opposition from conservative officials and scholars who opposed his New Policies (Xin Fa). These policies included land reform, state monopolies on salt and iron, and military reorganization. The resistance from the literati and established bureaucracy was intense, and Wang Anshi's position, though still strong, was increasingly precarious. His reforms aimed to strengthen the state financially and militarily but were seen by many as violations of Confucian principles and threats to the scholar-official class.
key_figures: Sima Guang (Conservative official, opponent of reforms)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, compiled earlier but relevant)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty, compiled in Yuan Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Song political history

events.certainty_note: Wang Anshi's tenure and the opposition to his policies are well-documented in Song historical records, though specific events in 1076 are not individually highlighted.

events.title: Wang Anshi resigns as Chief Minister of Song China
events.description: In 1076, Wang Anshi resigned from his position as Chief Minister of the Northern Song Dynasty, ending his period of greatest influence. The resignation came after years of mounting opposition from conservative officials and scholars who opposed his New Policies. Though Wang Anshi would briefly return to office later, this resignation marked the beginning of the end of his reform program. His departure allowed conservative forces, led by figures like Sima Guang, to regain influence and gradually dismantle many of his initiatives.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Song political history

events.certainty_note: Wang Anshi's resignation in 1076 is well-documented in Song historical records.
cross_references: 1086-001 (Sima Guang's ascendancy after Emperor Shenzong's death)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Synod of Worms (1076-001) is sometimes overstated in older historiography as a 'victory' for Henry IV; modern scholarship emphasizes that the synod had limited immediate effect and that Gregory VII's counter-excommunication was far more consequential. The synod's declarations were largely ignored by the papacy and had little support outside Germany. Additionally, the narrative of Seljuk consolidation in Anatolia (1076-004) is sometimes presented as a simple military conquest, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex processes of settlement, accommodation, and gradual cultural transformation, not a sudden displacement. Wang Anshi's resignation (1076-006) is sometimes dated to 1074 or 1076 depending on the source; the exact date is debated among scholars of Song history.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1076 is rich for European events, particularly the Investiture Controversy, which is extensively covered in contemporary chronicles and papal records. However, the sources are heavily weighted toward the perspective of the papacy and the German church; secular and popular perspectives are largely absent. For East Asia, the Song historical records are detailed but were compiled centuries later and reflect the biases of later dynasties. Scandinavian sources for Danish succession are sparse and often contradictory. The Seljuk consolidation in Anatolia is poorly documented in contemporary sources; most information comes from Byzantine chronicles (which are hostile) or later Islamic sources. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving documentation for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Synod of Worms prompted Gregory VII's counter-excommunication
Gregory VII's excommunication forced Henry IV to seek reconciliation at Canossa
Sweyn II's death initiated succession disputes that shaped Danish politics for a decade
Opposition to Wang Anshi's New Policies led to his resignation
The Seljuk victory at Manzikert enabled the consolidation described in 1076
